points.text: Add per‑IP and per‑account rate limiting on the password‑reset endpoint (e.g., 5 requests/hour).
Introduce a CAPTCHA challenge after a threshold of reset attempts to block bots.
Enforce short, single‑use reset tokens with strict expiration (e.g., 10 min) and store them in a fast cache.
Offload email sending to a background queue (e.g., RabbitMQ, SQS) so the API returns quickly.
Cache recent reset token lookups in Redis to avoid DB hits for duplicate attempts.
Add DB indexes on the reset token and user_id columns to speed validation queries.
Increase DB connection pool size and enable read‑replicas for read‑heavy validation queries.
Implement circuit breaker and exponential back‑off on client retries to prevent overload loops.
Create a composite index on password_reset_requests(user_id, request_time) to speed recent‑request lookups.
Add a unique index on the reset_token column to prevent full table scans during token validation.
Offload password‑reset email sending to a background job queue to reduce request‑time DB load.
Implement per‑IP/user rate limiting in the application, using a fast store (e.g., Redis) to avoid DB hits.
Enable and tune connection pooling; increase max connections only after monitoring actual usage.
Deploy a read replica and direct analytics or status queries there to keep the primary free for writes.
Archive or partition old reset request rows (e.g., >30 days) to keep the active table small and indexable.
Use READ COMMITTED isolation for inserts to avoid unnecessary row‑level locks during spikes.
Add CAPTCHA or email‑verification steps on the reset form to deter automated abuse.
Adjust DB parameters (work_mem, autovacuum settings) to handle higher insert rates and keep statistics fresh.
Deploy a rate‑limit (e.g., nginx limit_req_zone) per IP or per account to cap password‑reset POSTs.
Enable HTTP 429 responses for excess requests; clients back off instead of retrying aggressively.
Front‑end with a CDN or edge cache that serves static reset pages, reducing DB hits.
Move reset‑email generation to an asynchronous worker queue (e.g., RabbitMQ) so DB isn’t blocked.
Increase Linux file‑descriptor limit (ulimit -n) and TCP backlog (net.core.somaxconn) to accept more concurrent connections.
Turn on SYN cookies (net.ipv4.tcp_syncookies=1) and reduce SYN‑retry count to mitigate SYN‑flood abuse.
Tune TCP retransmission timeout (net.ipv4.tcp_retries2) to fail faster, preventing long hangs during DB latency spikes.
Add a connection pool for DB (e.g., pgbouncer) and enable query caching to lower load.
Enable rate limiting on the password‑reset endpoint (e.g., 5 requests per IP per hour).
Add CAPTCHA or hCaptcha to the reset form to block automated abuse.
Enforce per‑account request throttling (e.g., max 3 resets per 24 h).
Shorten reset token TTL (e.g., 15 minutes) and store tokens hashed in the DB.
Queue reset email generation via a background worker to decouple DB load.
Cache recent reset tokens in Redis (write‑through) to reduce DB reads.
Verify and add missing index on the reset token lookup column for fast queries.
Deploy a read replica for password‑reset lookups to offload the primary DB.
Implement a circuit‑breaker on the reset service; return 429 when overload detected.
Enable exponential backoff on client retries and set alerts on retry spikes.
